items.question: How does SVD compare to eigendecomposition of a square matrix A?
items.answer: SVD always exists for any m×n A, uses non-negative singular values and orthogonal U, V. Eigendecomposition requires square A and may involve complex eigenvalues and non-orthogonal eigenvectors. For symmetric A, eigenvalues match singular values up to sign and eigenvectors match singular vectors.

items.question: SVD of X vs eigendecomposition of covariance XᵀX/n—what is the practical difference?
items.answer: Both yield the same principal directions for centered data, but SVD on X avoids forming the n×n covariance explicitly—better when feature dimension is huge but sample count is moderate, and more numerically stable in many cases.

items.question: What happens to reconstruction error when you increase k in truncated SVD?
items.answer: Cause: you retain more singular values and associated directions. Effect: Frobenius error ||A - A_k||_F decreases monotonically; Eckart–Young guarantees A_k is the best rank-k approximation at each k.

items.question: What happens if A is rank-deficient?
items.answer: Cause: dependent rows/columns make some σᵢ exactly zero. Effect: SVD still exists; zero singular values reveal null directions; pseudoinverse uses reciprocals of non-zero σᵢ only.
